space.title: The Sun: Our Local Star
space.blurb: A gargantuan ball of glowing gas at the heart of our solar system, the Sun pours out light and warmth that almost every living thing on Earth depends upon.
paragraphs: At the very centre of our solar system sits the Sun, a gargantuan ball of burning gas so enormous — so utterly vast in size — that more than a million Earths could fit inside it. To us it looks like a small golden disc in the daytime sky, yet it is the apex of our cosmic neighbourhood, sitting right at the very top and centre while eight planets, including our own, circle endlessly around it. Everything in the solar system is held in place by the Sun's powerful pull.

The Sun is incandescent — glowing white-hot with fierce heat and light — with a surface temperature of around 5,500 degrees Celsius. Deep in its core the temperature climbs to an immense fifteen million degrees, a heat so vast it is almost impossible to imagine. There, tiny particles are squeezed together with such force that they release astonishing amounts of energy, in a process scientists call nuclear fusion. This is the engine that keeps our star shining.

That energy travels outwards as a radiant flood of light — beaming brightly in every direction — crossing 150 million kilometres of empty space to reach us in just over eight minutes. The Sun is the most luminous object we ever see, so brilliantly bright that we must never look straight at it. Its light gives us daytime, warms our planet, and allows plants to grow, which in turn feeds nearly every creature alive.

The Sun is far from calm and still. Its surface is volatile — restless and likely to erupt without warning — throwing out towering loops of fiery gas and sudden, explosive bursts called solar flares. These eruptions are so powerful that they can disturb satellites and radios on Earth, and they paint beautiful glowing curtains of light, the auroras, near our planet's poles.

The Sun's work is ceaseless — never stopping, never pausing for even a moment. It has been shining steadily for about four and a half billion years and has enough fuel to keep burning for billions more. Without this faithful, blazing star, Earth would be a frozen, lifeless rock drifting in the dark. The Sun is, quite simply, the reason our world is alive.

image.caption: The Sun captured in ultraviolet light by NASA's Solar Dynamics Observatory
image.credit: NASA / SDO / Wikimedia Commons / Public domain

space.title: The Moon: Earth's Companion
space.blurb: Our nearest neighbour in space, the Moon is a desolate, silent world that lights up our night sky and has fascinated people for thousands of years.
paragraphs: The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite, a rocky companion that has circled our planet for billions of years. Hanging in the night sky, it often looks serene — peaceful and perfectly calm — gliding silently above us. Yet this gentle glow hides a strange and dramatic history, for the Moon was most likely born when a young Earth was struck by another world the size of Mars, flinging a cloud of rock into space that slowly gathered together.

The Moon's surface is utterly desolate — empty, bare, and without a single living thing. There is no air to breathe, no water to drink, and no weather of any kind. Its grey plains are covered in fine dust and scarred by thousands of craters, each one a wound left by a crashing rock from space. Because there is no wind or rain to wear them away, these craters can stay almost pristine — clean and unspoiled — for billions of years.

Although the Moon looks luminous — softly shining in the dark — it makes no light of its own at all. Instead it works like an enormous mirror, catching sunlight and bouncing it back towards Earth. As the Moon travels around us, we see different amounts of its sunlit side, which is why it appears to change shape, growing from a thin sliver into a full silver circle and shrinking away again over about a month.

Standing on the Moon would feel eerie — strange and unsettling in a way that sends a shiver down your spine. The sky is always pitch black, even in daytime, and the silence is total because sound cannot travel without air. In 1969, astronauts first walked on this venerable world — one so ancient and worthy of respect that it had watched over the Earth long before any human existed — and their footprints still sit undisturbed in the dust today.

The Moon does far more than decorate our nights. Its gentle pull tugs on Earth's oceans, creating the tides that rise and fall along our coasts, and it helps keep our planet's tilt steady so the seasons stay reliable. On a clear, tranquil evening — calm, still, and quiet — the Moon remains the most beautiful sight in the sky, a faithful friend that has lit humanity's way through countless ages.

image.caption: The full Moon, showing its cratered surface in brilliant detail
image.credit: Gregory H. Revera / Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 3.0

space.title: Mars: The Red Planet
space.blurb: A cold, rust-coloured desert world, Mars is the planet most like our own — and the place where humans may one day build their first home beyond Earth.
paragraphs: Mars is the fourth planet from the Sun and the next one out beyond Earth. People have always noticed its strange crimson colour — a deep, rich red — glowing in the night sky, which is why ancient peoples named it after their god of war. That red comes from iron in the soil that has slowly rusted over billions of years, covering the whole planet in a coat of reddish dust, almost as if Mars had gone rusty.

Today Mars is a desolate world — empty, dry, and silent — with no rivers, no forests, and no sign of life. Its landscape is bleak — bare, cold, and joyless to look at — a frozen desert where temperatures can drop to minus 120 degrees Celsius on a winter night. The thin air is poisonous to humans, and great dust storms can sweep across the planet, making Mars a deeply hostile place — unwelcoming and dangerous to any visitor.

Yet Mars is also a world of vast and astonishing landscapes — enormous in scale and stretching further than the eye can see. It boasts the tallest volcano in the entire solar system, Olympus Mons, which rises almost three times higher than Mount Everest. It also has a canyon so huge, Valles Marineris, that it would stretch right across an entire continent on Earth, dwarfing anything our own planet can offer.

Scientists believe that long ago Mars was warmer and wetter, with rivers, lakes, and perhaps even a sparse — thinly scattered and far apart — covering of simple living things. Today, robot explorers called rovers trundle across its surface, drilling into rocks and searching for any clue that life once existed there. Frozen water still hides beneath the soil and at the icy poles.

Many scientists dream of sending astronauts to Mars within our lifetimes. Living there would demand people who are remarkably resilient — tough and able to recover from hardship — surviving inside sealed habitats, growing their own food, and shielding themselves from dangerous radiation. If humans ever succeed, the Red Planet could become the first world beyond Earth that our species has ever called home.

image.caption: Mars in true colour, showing its characteristic red surface from orbit
image.credit: ESA / Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 3.0 IGO

space.title: Saturn and Its Rings
space.blurb: Crowned by the most magnificent rings in the solar system, the giant planet Saturn is one of the most breathtaking sights a telescope can reveal.
paragraphs: Saturn is the sixth planet from the Sun and the second largest in the solar system, a colossal ball of gas so light that, if you could find an ocean big enough, the whole planet would float. But it is not Saturn's size that makes people gasp — it is the magnificence of its rings, a grandeur and splendour so striking that the planet is instantly recognisable to almost everyone on Earth.

Those rings are truly majestic — grand, noble, and awe-inspiring to behold — encircling the planet in broad, sweeping bands of brilliant white. Through a telescope they look like a smooth, solid hoop, but in reality they are made of countless billions of chunks of ice and rock, ranging from tiny grains to lumps as big as houses. Each piece orbits Saturn in its own path, all of them glistening — sparkling and gleaming brightly — as they catch the distant sunlight.

Seen up close by passing spacecraft, the rings are shimmering — glowing with a soft, wavering, watery light — and divided into thousands of fine, delicate bands. This makes Saturn look wonderfully ornate — richly decorated and beautifully detailed — as though some cosmic artist had wrapped the planet in an elaborate ribbon. Scientists think the rings may be the shattered remains of a moon or comet that wandered too close and was torn apart.

Saturn itself is a vast and stormy giant — enormous beyond imagining and many times wider than Earth. Powerful winds race around it faster than any hurricane on our planet, and a strange six-sided storm, shaped like a perfect hexagon, swirls forever around its north pole. The planet is also surrounded by a busy family of more than a hundred moons, including Titan, a misty world with rivers and lakes made of liquid gas.

When the Cassini spacecraft spent years studying Saturn, it sent back spectacular pictures — dramatic, jaw-dropping views that thrilled scientists and schoolchildren alike. Saturn reminds us that the universe is full of beauty waiting to be discovered, and for many young stargazers, the first glimpse of those glorious rings through a telescope is a moment they never forget.

image.caption: Saturn and its magnificent rings, photographed by the Cassini spacecraft
image.credit: NASA / JPL / Space Science Institute / Wikimedia Commons / Public domain

space.title: Black Holes
space.blurb: The strangest objects in the universe, black holes are places where gravity grows so powerful that not even light can escape their grasp.
paragraphs: A black hole is one of the most extraordinary things in the entire universe — a place where gravity becomes so immense, so unimaginably strong, that nothing at all can break free of its pull. Not even light, the fastest thing that exists, can escape once it falls too close. This makes a black hole truly invisible, a patch of pure darkness hiding silently among the stars.

Black holes remain deeply mysterious — puzzling and full of secrets that scientists are still struggling to understand. They usually form when a giant star runs out of fuel and collapses inwards, crushing an enormous amount of material into a single point smaller than a grain of sand. The result is an object so heavy that it bends the very fabric of space and time around itself, warping everything nearby.

A black hole can be voracious — greedily devouring everything within reach — pulling in gas, dust, and even whole stars that drift too near. As this material spirals in, it whirls around faster and faster and heats up until it blazes with light, forming a glowing ring that lets astronomers spot a black hole even though the hole itself stays obscure — hidden, dark, and impossible to see directly.

At the centre of our own galaxy lurks a gargantuan black hole — colossal beyond all comparison — called Sagittarius A-star. It weighs as much as four million Suns, yet it is so far away that it poses no danger to us at all. Its ominous presence — threatening and full of menace — sits quietly at the heart of the Milky Way, holding billions of stars in its powerful grip.

Studying black holes takes persistent effort — steady, determined work that never gives up — and in 2019 scientists finally captured the first ever photograph of one. By linking telescopes all over the world into a single giant 'eye', they revealed a fiery orange ring circling a perfect circle of black. It was proof that these astonishing objects, once only imagined, truly exist out there in the dark.

image.caption: The first ever photograph of a black hole, at the centre of galaxy Messier 87
image.credit: Event Horizon Telescope Collaboration / Wikimedia Commons / CC BY 4.0

space.title: Comets: Icy Wanderers
space.region: The Edge of the Solar System
space.blurb: Dirty snowballs from the frozen edges of the solar system, comets blaze into glowing visitors when they swing close to the Sun.
paragraphs: A comet is sometimes called a dirty snowball, and it really is rather like one — a lump of ice, dust, and rock left over from the birth of the solar system. Most comets spend their lives in the deep, dark, freezing edges of space, far beyond the planets. But every now and then, one is nudged onto a long journey inwards towards the Sun, and that is when the magic begins.

As a comet nears the Sun, its ice heats up and turns to gas, wrapping the comet in a glowing cloud and streaming out behind it as a luminous tail — brightly shining and easy to see. This dazzling tail can stretch for millions of kilometres and always points away from the Sun, blown back by the Sun's constant wind, like a flag fluttering in a cosmic breeze.

A bright comet in the night sky is an ephemeral wonder — lasting only a short time before it fades away. For a few precious weeks it may hang among the stars, sometimes shining with a vivid glow — bright, bold, and richly coloured — before swinging back out into the darkness. Some comets will not return for hundreds or even thousands of years, making each visit a rare and special event.

People have watched comets for thousands of years, and these visitors are venerable signs in the sky — ancient and treated with deep respect — that once filled our ancestors with wonder and sometimes fear. The most famous, Halley's Comet, returns about every seventy-six years, and even a faint comet can be spotted as a soft glimmer — a weak, flickering shine — low on the horizon.

Comets are also swift — moving with tremendous speed — racing through space at many kilometres every second as they whip around the Sun. Their orbits can be capricious — unpredictable and quick to change — easily tugged off course by the gravity of giant planets like Jupiter. Scientists treasure comets because their frozen ice is almost unchanged since the solar system began, making each one a deep-frozen message from the dawn of time.

image.caption: Comet Hale-Bopp blazing across the night sky with its brilliant twin tails
image.credit: E. Kolmhofer, H. Raab / Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 3.0

space.title: Rockets: Escaping Earth
space.blurb: Roaring with unimaginable power, rockets are the only machines mighty enough to lift people and spacecraft beyond the pull of our planet.
paragraphs: To leave Earth and reach space, a spacecraft must travel incredibly fast — about forty thousand kilometres per hour — to break free of our planet's powerful gravity. Only one machine is mighty enough to do this: the rocket. With a thunderous roar — a deep, booming sound that shakes the ground and rattles your chest — a rocket tears itself off the launch pad and climbs towards the sky.

When the engines ignite, hot gases blast downwards with enormous force, and the rocket surged upwards in response — rose suddenly and powerfully — pushing against nothing but the gas it throws out behind it. This clever idea is wonderfully ingenious — clever, original, and brilliantly designed — and it is the same reason a balloon zooms across a room when you let the air rush out.

Many rockets are gargantuan — colossal and towering in size — standing taller than a tall building when they sit on the launch pad. The famous Saturn V, which carried astronauts to the Moon, was as tall as a thirty-storey tower and weighed nearly three thousand tonnes, most of which was fuel. Almost all of that giant machine is simply tanks holding the fuel needed to escape the Earth.

A rocket must be extraordinarily robust — strong, sturdy, and able to survive enormous strain — because launch is a violent, fiery, shaking ordeal. Engineers build them in sections called stages: as each stage empties its fuel, it falls away, making the rocket lighter so the rest can keep climbing. Modern rockets can even fly their lower stages back down to land upright, ready to be used again.

Sending humans into space is an audacious adventure — daring, bold, and bravely ambitious — and it takes years of careful planning to do it safely. Yet thanks to rockets, we have lifted satellites, telescopes, robot explorers, and brave astronauts aloft — high up into the sky and far beyond it. Every launch is a reminder of how far human cleverness and courage can carry us.

image.caption: The Saturn V rocket lifting off on 16 July 1969, carrying the Apollo 11 crew to the Moon

space.title: Astronauts: Living in Space
space.blurb: Floating weightless far above the Earth, astronauts are the brave explorers who live and work in the most extreme environment humans have ever entered.
paragraphs: An astronaut is a person trained to travel and work in space, one of the most dangerous and demanding jobs anywhere. To become one takes years of study and practice, because space is utterly unforgiving. These valiant explorers — brave and heroic in the face of great danger — must be ready for emergencies that could happen hundreds of kilometres above the Earth, with no way to simply call for help.

Life in space is strange and difficult, and astronauts must be remarkably resilient — tough and able to cope with hardship — to survive it. With no gravity to hold them down, everything floats, including the astronauts themselves. They must strap themselves into bed to sleep, drink from sealed pouches so the water does not drift away in wobbling bubbles, and exercise for hours every day to stop their muscles and bones from growing weak.

Every task an astronaut performs is meticulous — done with great care and attention to the smallest detail — because in space a tiny mistake can become a serious danger. Spacewalks outside the craft are especially perilous, so astronauts rehearse each movement again and again in giant water tanks back on Earth until they can do it perfectly, even while wearing a bulky, stiff spacesuit.

Out in orbit, astronauts must stay vigilant — alert and watchful at all times — checking their equipment, monitoring the air they breathe, and keeping a constant eye on the systems that keep them alive. They are resolute people — firmly determined and not easily put off — who carry on with their work calmly even when far from family, friends, and everything familiar about home.

Becoming an astronaut is an audacious dream — bold and daringly ambitious — yet ordinary people from all over the world have achieved it through courageous, determined effort. Brave, fearless, and willing to face the unknown, astronauts carry out experiments, repair satellites, and gaze down at our beautiful blue planet, gathering knowledge that helps everyone back on Earth understand the universe a little better.

image.caption: An astronaut on a spacewalk, floating in the vacuum of space above the Earth

space.title: The International Space Station
space.blurb: A floating laboratory the size of a football pitch, the Space Station is the largest machine humans have ever built in orbit — and a home shared by many nations.
paragraphs: Circling about four hundred kilometres above our heads is the International Space Station, or ISS, the largest structure humans have ever placed in space. Roughly as long as a football pitch, it races around the Earth at nearly twenty-eight thousand kilometres per hour, completing a full lap of our planet every ninety minutes. From the ground it looks like a bright, fast-moving star sliding across the night sky.

The Space Station is an ingenious creation — clever, original, and brilliantly worked out — built piece by piece in orbit over many years. It was not the work of one country alone but a collective effort — shared by many people and nations working together — with parts launched from around the world and joined high above the Earth. Astronauts from many different lands have lived and worked aboard it side by side.

Aboard the station, work is ceaseless — never stopping, going on day and night without pause. Because the ISS orbits so fast, the crew see a sunrise and a sunset roughly every forty-five minutes, so they keep to careful clocks instead. The station's machinery is robust — strong, sturdy, and built to keep working reliably — recycling the air the astronauts breathe and even turning their wastewater back into clean drinking water.

Everything aboard runs in a methodical way — carefully ordered, step by step, and well planned out. Astronauts follow detailed timetables for their experiments, exercise, repairs, and meals, all floating weightlessly as they go. The station is really a giant laboratory, where innovative research — fresh, inventive, and full of new ideas — explores how plants grow, how materials behave, and how the human body copes far from the gravity of home.

High above the swirling blue vault of the sky — the great curved dome stretching overhead — the Space Station glides on, a shining symbol of what people can build when they cooperate. For more than twenty years it has been continuously lived in, never once empty of humans. It stands as proof that, working together, the nations of Earth can achieve something truly extraordinary among the stars.

image.caption: The International Space Station photographed from the departing Space Shuttle Atlantis

space.title: The Milky Way Galaxy
space.blurb: Our home in the universe, the Milky Way is a vast spinning city of hundreds of billions of stars — and our Sun is just one tiny resident among them.
paragraphs: On a clear, dark night, far from city lights, you might see a pale, milky band of light arching across the sky. That faint glow is our own galaxy, the Milky Way, seen edge-on from the inside. A galaxy is a vast collection of stars — enormous beyond anything we can truly picture — and ours holds somewhere between one and four hundred billion of them, all bound together by gravity.

The Milky Way is so immense — so unimaginably large — that light, which crosses our entire solar system in mere hours, takes a hundred thousand years to travel from one side of it to the other. It is shaped like a flattened spinning disc with great curving arms, and it is packed with luminous stars — brilliantly shining points of light — along with glowing clouds of gas and dust where brand-new stars are being born.

If you could rise far above the galaxy and look back, you would see a breathtaking panorama — a wide, sweeping view stretching in every direction — of glittering spiral arms slowly turning like a colossal cosmic pinwheel. The sheer magnificence of it — its grandeur, beauty, and splendour — has inspired stargazers, painters, and storytellers for as long as human beings have looked upward in wonder.

Our own Sun sits about halfway out along one of these spiral arms, never reaching the bright, crowded zenith — the very highest, central point — of the galaxy where stars cluster most thickly. Together with all its planets, the Sun sweeps around the galaxy's centre once every two hundred and thirty million years, a single galactic 'year' so long that we have completed only a handful since the Sun was born.

Beyond the Milky Way lies an even greater realm — a whole kingdom or domain — of countless other galaxies, stretching on for billions of light years. The universe is teeming with them — utterly crowded and overflowing — each one a swirling island of stars like our own. When we gaze up at the Milky Way, we are looking at our true home address in the universe: one ordinary star, in one spiral arm, of one galaxy, among billions.

image.caption: The Milky Way arching across the desert night sky, revealing our home galaxy edge-on
image.credit: Haap Media Ltd / Wikimedia Commons / CC BY-SA 3.0
